statement: Madagascar was first permanently settled by Austronesian peoples from present-day Indonesia, not from nearby mainland Africa.
quizQuestion: Where did Madagascar's first permanent settlers originally come from?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Madagascar was first permanently settled during or before the mid-first millennium CE by Austronesian peoples arriving on outrigger canoes from present-day Indonesia — over 6,000 km across the Indian Ocean, despite Africa being just 400 km away.
wowFactor: Madagascar's first settlers sailed 6,000 km from Indonesia on outrigger canoes, completely bypassing nearby Africa!

variants.question: Which island nation was first settled by Austronesian peoples from Indonesia?
Austronesian peoples crossed the Indian Ocean on outrigger canoes to settle which large island?
True or false: Madagascar was first settled by Bantu peoples from East Africa.
Madagascar's first permanent settlers were ___ peoples from present-day Indonesia.

visualDescription: An outrigger canoe with Austronesian sailors crosses a vast turquoise ocean under starlight, with a lush green island rising on the horizon.

statement: Over 90% of Madagascar's wildlife is found nowhere else on Earth, making it one of the planet's most unique biodiversity hotspots.
quizQuestion: What percentage of Madagascar's wildlife is endemic — found nowhere else?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Madagascar's long isolation — splitting from Africa around 180 million years ago and from India about 90 million years ago — allowed its plants and animals to evolve in complete seclusion, producing over 90% endemic species.
wowFactor: Nine out of ten species on Madagascar exist absolutely nowhere else on the planet — it's like a parallel evolution experiment!

variants.question: Which island country has over 90% endemic wildlife due to millions of years of isolation?
Madagascar split from Africa {180} million years ago — what did this isolation produce?
True or false: Madagascar is one of the world's 17 megadiverse countries.
Madagascar split from Africa during the Early Jurassic, around ___ million years ago.

visualDescription: A lemur perches on a baobab tree in a lush Madagascan rainforest, surrounded by chameleons and exotic birds found nowhere else on Earth.

statement: Madagascar is the world's fourth-largest island and the second-largest island country.
quizQuestion: What is Madagascar's rank among the world's largest islands?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Madagascar is the world's fourth-largest island after Greenland, New Guinea, and Borneo. It is also the second-largest island country after Indonesia.
wowFactor: Madagascar is so huge it's the fourth-largest island on Earth — and the second-largest country that's entirely islands!

variants.question: Which is the world's fourth-largest island?
True or false: Madagascar is the world's largest island country.
After Greenland, New Guinea, and Borneo, what is the next largest island?
Madagascar is the world's ___-largest island.

visualDescription: A satellite view of the enormous island of Madagascar off the southeast coast of Africa, its red soil and green forests visible from orbit.

statement: A single ship passing through the Panama Canal uses an average of 200 million litres (52 million US gallons) of fresh water.
quizQuestion: How many litres of fresh water does one ship use passing through the Panama Canal?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, an average of 200,000,000 litres of fresh water is used in a single passing of a ship through the canal's lock system, which lifts vessels to Gatun Lake at 26 metres above sea level. The canal is now threatened by low water levels during droughts.
wowFactor: Every single ship through the Panama Canal guzzles 200 million litres of fresh water — enough to fill 80 Olympic swimming pools!

variants.question: Which waterway uses 200 million litres of fresh water per ship transit?
The Panama Canal's lock system lifts ships to an artificial lake at what height above sea level?
True or false: the Panama Canal is threatened by low water levels during droughts.
Ships in the Panama Canal are lifted to ___, an artificial freshwater lake.

visualDescription: A massive container ship squeezes through narrow concrete locks as millions of litres of water cascade and churn around its hull.

statement: France originally began building the Panama Canal in 1881 but abandoned the project in 1889 due to engineering problems and high worker mortality.
quizQuestion: Which country first attempted to build the Panama Canal before abandoning it?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, France began work on the canal in 1881 but stopped in 1889 due to engineering problems and a devastating worker mortality rate. The US took over the project in 1904 and successfully opened it in 1914.
wowFactor: France tried to build the Panama Canal first and failed catastrophically — the US picked up the pieces 15 years later!

variants.question: Which massive engineering project did France abandon in 1889 before the US completed it?
France quit building the Panama Canal in 1889 partly due to what devastating problem?
True or false: the United States built the Panama Canal entirely from scratch.
The Panama Canal was inaugurated on 15 August ___.

visualDescription: Rusted French-era excavation machinery lies abandoned in a jungle trench, overgrown with vines, as workers in the distance dig under a tropical sun.

statement: The Panama Canal is an 82-kilometre (51-mile) artificial waterway connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans across the Isthmus of Panama.
quizQuestion: How long is the Panama Canal in kilometres?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Panama Canal stretches 82 kilometres across the narrowest point of the Isthmus of Panama. Its construction was one of the largest and most difficult engineering projects ever undertaken, and it has reshaped global trade since opening in 1914.
wowFactor: Just 82 kilometres of canal saves ships from a 15,000-km detour around the entire continent of South America!

variants.question: Which famous waterway is 82 kilometres long?
The Panama Canal connects the Caribbean Sea with which ocean?
True or false: the Panama Canal is now managed by the Panamanian government.
The Panama Canal cuts across the narrowest point of the Isthmus of ___.

visualDescription: An aerial view of the Panama Canal slicing through lush jungle, with a container ship gliding between concrete lock walls toward the distant Pacific.

statement: Norway has the world's largest sovereign wealth fund, valued at US$2.1 trillion, built from its petroleum revenues.
quizQuestion: Which country owns the world's largest sovereign wealth fund at $2.1 trillion?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Norway's sovereign wealth fund is the largest in the world at US$2.1 trillion. It was built from the country's petroleum and natural gas revenues, and Norway is the world's largest per-capita producer of oil and gas outside the Middle East.
wowFactor: Norway saved its oil money so well that every single citizen is technically a millionaire on paper through the national wealth fund!

variants.question: The world's largest sovereign wealth fund, worth $2.1 trillion, belongs to which Nordic country?

statement: The unified Kingdom of Norway was established in 872 CE and has existed continuously for over 1,150 years.
quizQuestion: In what year was the unified Kingdom of Norway established?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the unified kingdom of Norway was established in 872 as a merger of petty kingdoms and has existed continuously since — making it one of the oldest continuously existing kingdoms in Europe.
wowFactor: Norway has been a unified kingdom since 872 CE — it's been around for over 1,150 years without a break!

variants.question: Which Nordic country was unified as a kingdom in 872 CE?
Norway was part of a personal union with which country from 1814 to 1905?

visualDescription: A Viking king in a fur-trimmed cloak raises a sword on a Norwegian cliff overlooking fjords, uniting petty kingdoms under one banner in 872 CE.

statement: Norway's indigenous Sami people have their own parliament and a degree of self-determination over traditional territories through the Finnmark Act.
quizQuestion: Which indigenous people have their own parliament within Norway?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Sami people have a certain amount of self-determination and influence over traditional territories through the Sami Parliament and the Finnmark Act, reflecting Norway's commitment to indigenous rights within its Nordic model.
wowFactor: Norway's indigenous Sami people have their very own parliament — one of the few indigenous groups in the world with that level of political power!

visualDescription: A modern parliament chamber decorated with Sami patterns and reindeer motifs, where representatives in traditional dress debate under warm Nordic light.

statement: The Bermuda Triangle has no evidence of unusual disappearance activity — the U.S. government and scientists attribute incidents to natural phenomena and human error.
quizQuestion: What have investigations found about Bermuda Triangle disappearances?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, extensive investigations by the U.S. government and scientific organizations have found no evidence of unusual activity in the Bermuda Triangle. Reported incidents are attributed to natural phenomena, human error, and misinterpretation.
wowFactor: The 'mystery' of the Bermuda Triangle is itself a myth — official investigations found absolutely nothing unusual happening there!

variants.question: Which famous region's 'mystery' was debunked by the U.S. government as natural phenomena and human error?

statement: The Bermuda Triangle is a loosely defined region in the North Atlantic Ocean, roughly bounded by Florida, Bermuda, and Puerto Rico.
quizQuestion: Which three locations roughly form the corners of the Bermuda Triangle?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Bermuda Triangle — also known as the Devil's Triangle — is a loosely defined region in the North Atlantic, roughly bounded by Florida, Bermuda, and Puerto Rico. The region became the focus of an urban legend in the mid-20th century.
wowFactor: The Bermuda Triangle is also called the Devil's Triangle — but its boundaries aren't even officially defined!

variants.question: Florida, Bermuda, and Puerto Rico form the corners of which famous region?

statement: The Bermuda Triangle legend claims many aircraft, ships, and people have disappeared there under mysterious circumstances since the mid-20th century.
quizQuestion: When did the Bermuda Triangle urban legend begin gaining widespread attention?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Bermuda Triangle became the focus of an urban legend since the mid-20th century, suggesting mysterious disappearances of aircraft, ships, and people. However, reputable investigations have attributed all incidents to mundane causes.
wowFactor: The Bermuda Triangle 'mystery' is surprisingly recent — nobody thought anything weird was going on there until the 1950s and 60s!

variants.question: Which supposed 'mystery zone' became a pop culture phenomenon in the mid-20th century?

statement: Greenland is the world's largest island, yet it is an autonomous territory of Denmark with a population heavily reliant on Danish aid.
quizQuestion: Greenland is an autonomous territory of which European country?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greenland is the world's largest island and an autonomous territory of the Kingdom of Denmark. Despite its vast size, it is economically reliant on Danish aid averaging 5.4 billion kr. annually — over 20% of Greenland's GDP.
wowFactor: The world's largest island is technically owned by one of Europe's smallest countries — Denmark is 50 times smaller than Greenland!

variants.question: Which is the world's largest island?
Danish aid accounts for over what percentage of Greenland's GDP?
True or false: Greenland is part of the European Union.

visualDescription: The massive white ice sheet of Greenland dwarfs a tiny Danish flag planted on its coast, with colorful houses of Nuuk visible in the distance.

statement: Greenland has been inhabited at intervals over at least the last 4,500 years by circumpolar peoples who migrated from what is now Canada.
quizQuestion: For at least how many years has Greenland been intermittently inhabited?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greenland has been inhabited at intervals over at least the last 4,500 years by circumpolar peoples whose forebears migrated from what is now Canada. Norse settlers from Norway later arrived in the 10th century but disappeared by the late 15th century.
wowFactor: People have been living on the world's biggest ice sheet for 4,500 years — and Viking settlers who came later mysteriously vanished after 400 years!

variants.question: Which massive Arctic island has been intermittently inhabited for at least 4,500 years?

visualDescription: Ancient circumpolar hunters in fur parkas paddle kayaks along Greenland's icy coast, with snow-capped mountains and glaciers behind them.

statement: Kaffeklubben Island, off Greenland's northern coast, is the world's northernmost undisputed point of land.
quizQuestion: What is the world's northernmost undisputed point of land?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kaffeklubben Island off Greenland's northern coast is the world's northernmost undisputed point of land. Cape Morris Jesup on Greenland's main island was thought to hold the record until the 1960s.
wowFactor: The northernmost land on Earth is a tiny island off Greenland named after a coffee club — Kaffeklubben literally means 'Coffee Club Island'!

variants.question: Off which territory's coast is Kaffeklubben Island, the world's northernmost land?

statement: Pompeii was buried under 4 to 6 metres of volcanic ash and pumice when Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 AD, preserving the city as a frozen snapshot of Roman life.
quizQuestion: How deep was Pompeii buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pompeii was buried under 4 to 6 metres of volcanic ash and pumice in the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. This deep burial largely preserved the city, offering a unique snapshot of Roman life frozen at the moment of destruction.
wowFactor: Pompeii was buried so deep — up to 6 metres of ash — that the entire city was preserved like a time capsule for nearly 1,700 years!

variants.question: Which ancient city was buried under 4 to 6 metres of ash by Mount Vesuvius?
In what year did Mount Vesuvius erupt and bury Pompeii?
True or false: Pompeii is the only archaeological site providing a complete picture of an ancient Roman city.
Pompeii was buried by an eruption of Mount ___.

sensitivityNote: Involves mass death event — presented historically
visualDescription: Layers of grey volcanic ash bury Roman columns and rooftops, with Mount Vesuvius looming ominously in the background, smoke still rising from its crater.

statement: Archaeologists created plaster moulds of Pompeii's victims by filling the voids left by decayed human bodies preserved in hardened volcanic ash.
quizQuestion: How did archaeologists recreate the final poses of Pompeii's victims?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, organic remains including human bodies were interred in the ash at Pompeii. Their eventual decay left voids that archaeologists filled with plaster, creating haunting moulds of figures frozen in their final moments of life.
wowFactor: The famous 'frozen' figures of Pompeii aren't actual bodies — they're plaster casts poured into the exact hollows the victims left behind in hardened ash!

variants.question: At which ancient site did archaeologists create plaster casts from body-shaped voids in volcanic ash?
True or false: the plaster figures at Pompeii are actual preserved human bodies.
What decayed inside Pompeii's hardened ash to leave the voids archaeologists later filled?
Archaeologists at Pompeii created ___ of figures in their final moments by filling body-shaped voids.

sensitivityNote: Depicts death and body preservation — educational context
visualDescription: An archaeologist carefully pours white plaster into a hollow cavity in hardened grey volcanic ash, revealing the shape of a crouching figure.

statement: Pompeii's walls are covered in graffiti providing rare examples of Vulgar Latin — the everyday spoken language that evolved into the modern Romance languages.
quizQuestion: What rare linguistic treasure was found carved on Pompeii's walls?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the numerous graffiti carved on Pompeii's walls provide a wealth of examples of Vulgar Latin — the colloquial spoken language of ordinary Romans, which contrasts sharply with the formal classical Latin of literary writers and eventually evolved into the Romance languages.
wowFactor: Pompeii's ancient graffiti captures how Romans actually talked — basically the text messages of 79 AD, and the ancestor of Italian, Spanish, and French!

variants.question: Which destroyed Roman city preserved rare examples of everyday spoken Latin on its walls?
Vulgar Latin found in Pompeii's graffiti contrasts with what type of Latin used by writers?
True or false: Pompeii receives approximately 2.5 million visitors annually.
Pompeii's graffiti provides examples of ___ Latin, the everyday spoken form of the language.

visualDescription: A crumbling Roman wall covered in scratched Latin graffiti, with humorous scrawls and love notes visible among the faded red plaster.

statement: The Titanic's lifeboats could hold only 1,178 people — roughly half the passengers on board — yet were lowered at an average of just 60% capacity.
quizQuestion: On average, how full were Titanic's lifeboats when lowered during the sinking?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Titanic's 20 lifeboats could hold 1,178 people — roughly half the passengers on board and a third of full capacity. Tragically, the lifeboats that were lowered carried an average of only 60% of their rated capacity, leaving hundreds of empty seats.
wowFactor: Hundreds of Titanic passengers died needlessly — the lifeboats that launched were only 60% full, with empty seats going into the freezing Atlantic!

variants.question: On which ship were lifeboats lowered at only 60% capacity during a famous sinking?
How many people could Titanic's lifeboats hold in total?
True or false: the Titanic carried more lifeboats than regulations required.
The Titanic carried ___ lifeboats, including 4 collapsible boats.

sensitivityNote: Involves mass death at sea — presented historically
visualDescription: A half-empty wooden lifeboat drifts in a dark icy ocean at night, with the glowing stern of the Titanic tilting in the background.

statement: The Titanic sank on its very first voyage, and it was the second time White Star Line had lost a ship on a maiden voyage — the first being RMS Tayleur in 1854.
quizQuestion: Which White Star Line ship was lost on a maiden voyage 58 years before the Titanic?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Titanic's sinking on its maiden voyage was actually the second time White Star Line had suffered such a loss. RMS Tayleur was lost on her maiden voyage in 1854 — a grim historical coincidence spanning nearly six decades.
wowFactor: The Titanic wasn't even the first White Star Line ship to sink on its maiden voyage — they'd already lost the RMS Tayleur in 1854!

statement: The Titanic's first-class section featured a gymnasium, swimming pool, Turkish bath, fine restaurants, and a high-powered radiotelegraph for passenger use.
quizQuestion: What exotic spa feature did the Titanic offer its first-class passengers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Titanic's first-class accommodations were designed as the pinnacle of luxury, including a gymnasium, swimming pool, smoking rooms, fine restaurants, and a Victorian-style Turkish bath — all aboard a ship in 1912.
wowFactor: The Titanic had a full Turkish bath, swimming pool, and gymnasium on board — it was basically a floating five-star hotel in 1912!

statement: The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous empire in history, stretching from the Sea of Japan to Eastern Europe.
quizQuestion: What was the largest contiguous empire in history?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous empire in history, stretching from the Sea of Japan to Eastern Europe, including Siberia, the Indian subcontinent, the Iranian plateau, and the Levant — all connected by land.
wowFactor: The Mongol Empire stretched from Korea to Hungary — the largest connected land empire humanity has ever seen!

variants.question: Which empire held the record for the largest contiguous land territory in history?

True or false: the British Empire was larger than the Mongol Empire in contiguous territory.

sensitivityNote: Involves conquest and violence — presented historically
visualDescription: A vast map stretching from the Pacific to Europe glows gold under Mongol banners, with horsemen riding across endless steppe connecting two continents.

statement: The Mongol Empire enforced the Pax Mongolica, connecting East and West and enabling the exchange of trade, technologies, and ideas across Eurasia.
quizQuestion: What term describes the period of peace and trade the Mongol Empire enforced across Eurasia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Mongol Empire's vast transcontinental territory connected the East with the West and the Pacific to the Mediterranean in an enforced Pax Mongolica, allowing the exchange of trade, technologies, commodities, and ideologies across Eurasia.
wowFactor: The Mongols were history's greatest connectors — their empire linked China to Europe, enabling the flow of ideas, goods, and even diseases across continents!

variants.question: Which empire enforced the Pax Mongolica across Eurasia?

statement: By 1294, the Mongol Empire had fractured into four separate khanates: the Golden Horde, Chagatai Khanate, Ilkhanate, and the Yuan dynasty in China.
quizQuestion: Into how many khanates did the Mongol Empire fracture by 1294?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, by Kublai Khan's death in 1294, succession disputes had fractured the Mongol Empire into four separate khanates: the Golden Horde in the northwest, the Chagatai Khanate in Central Asia, the Ilkhanate in Iran, and the Yuan dynasty based in Beijing.
wowFactor: The mighty Mongol Empire didn't fall to outsiders — it tore itself apart into four rival khanates because Genghis Khan's grandchildren couldn't stop fighting over who was in charge!

variants.question: Which of the four Mongol successor states was based in China?

visualDescription: A great Mongol map shatters into four glowing fragments, each bearing the banner of a different khanate, drifting apart across Eurasia.

statement: The Spanish Inquisition was authorized by Pope Sixtus IV in 1478 and primarily targeted Jewish and Muslim converts to Catholicism suspected of secretly practicing their former faiths.
quizQuestion: Who authorized the establishment of the Spanish Inquisition in 1478?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Spanish Inquisition was authorized by Pope Sixtus IV in 1478, with the first inquisitors appointed by the future Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand II and Isabella I in 1480. It primarily targeted converts from Judaism and Islam suspected of heresy.
wowFactor: The Spanish Inquisition started as a religious purity test for Jewish and Muslim converts — and spiraled into one of history's most notorious institutions!

variants.question: Which institution was authorized by Pope Sixtus IV in 1478 to maintain Catholic orthodoxy in Spain?

The Spanish Inquisition was authorized in the year ___.

sensitivityNote: Religious persecution, forced conversion, execution — presented historically
visualDescription: A solemn papal document bearing a wax seal sits on a dark oak table, candlelight illuminating the decree that launched the Spanish Inquisition.

statement: An estimated 40,000 to 100,000 Jews were expelled from Spain in 1492, forced to convert to Catholicism or face death.
quizQuestion: In what year were tens of thousands of Jews expelled from Spain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, royal decrees in 1492 ordered Jews to convert to Catholicism, leave Castile, or face death. An estimated 40,000 to 100,000 Jews were expelled. Conversos who remained were subjected to blood purity statutes (limpieza de sangre), introducing racially-based discrimination lasting into the 20th century.
wowFactor: 1492 wasn't just the year Columbus sailed — it was also the year Spain expelled up to 100,000 Jews, one of history's largest forced exoduses!

variants.question: Which country expelled an estimated 40,000-100,000 Jews in 1492?
What discriminatory system subjected Jewish converts in Spain to racial purity tests?
True or false: Spain's blood purity statutes lasted into the 19th and 20th centuries.

sensitivityNote: Religious persecution, antisemitism, forced conversion, mass expulsion — presented historically and educationally
visualDescription: A long procession of families carrying bundles walks away from a Spanish city gate toward the coast, ships waiting in the harbor under a grey sky.

statement: The Spanish Inquisition prosecuted around 150,000 people over its history, of whom 3,000 to 5,000 were executed, mostly by burning at the stake.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many people were prosecuted by the Spanish Inquisition in total?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, modern estimates suggest around 150,000 people were prosecuted for various offences during the Spanish Inquisition. Of these, 3,000 to 5,000 were executed, particularly in the initial 50 years. Other punishments included public flogging, exile, galley enslavement, and property confiscation.
wowFactor: The Spanish Inquisition prosecuted 150,000 people — but 'only' executed 3-5% of them. Most faced punishments like galley enslavement or total property confiscation instead.

variants.question: Which institution prosecuted approximately 150,000 people over several centuries?

True or false: the Spanish Inquisition's executions were mostly carried out by burning at the stake.

sensitivityNote: Execution, torture, religious persecution — presented historically
visualDescription: A grim medieval courtroom with robed inquisitors behind a long oak bench, stacks of documents and a scales of justice dimly lit by candlelight.

statement: Armstrong's moonwalk was broadcast live to an estimated 600 million viewers — roughly one-fifth of the world's population in 1969.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many people watched the Apollo 11 moonwalk live on television?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Armstrong's moonwalk was broadcast live to an estimated 600 million viewers, roughly one-fifth of the world's population at the time, making it among the most-watched television events in history.
wowFactor: One in five humans alive on Earth stopped what they were doing to watch Neil Armstrong take his first step on the Moon — 600 million people, live!

variants.question: Which 1969 event was watched live by approximately 600 million people worldwide?

visualDescription: Families around the world huddle around grainy black-and-white television sets showing a ghostly astronaut stepping onto the lunar surface.

statement: Armstrong and Aldrin collected 21.5 kg of lunar material during Apollo 11, leading to the discovery of three previously unrecognized minerals.
quizQuestion: How many new minerals were discovered from Apollo 11's lunar samples?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Armstrong and Aldrin collected 21.5 kg of lunar material during their approximately two and a half hours walking on the Moon's surface. Analysis of these samples led to the identification of three previously unrecognized minerals.
wowFactor: The Moon rocks Armstrong brought back contained three minerals that had never been seen before — the Moon literally gave us brand-new science!

variants.question: Which space mission's samples led to the discovery of three new minerals?

statement: While Armstrong and Aldrin were still on the Moon, the Soviet probe Luna 15 crashed on the lunar surface on July 21 — a dramatic end to the Space Race.
quizQuestion: What happened to the Soviet probe Luna 15 while Apollo 11 astronauts were on the Moon?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Soviet Union's uncrewed lunar probe Luna 15 crashed on the Moon on July 21, while Armstrong and Aldrin were still on the lunar surface. It was a final dramatic symbol of the Space Race, which the US definitively won with Apollo 11.
wowFactor: While Armstrong was literally walking on the Moon, a Soviet probe crashed into it just hours later — the Space Race's most dramatic mic-drop moment!

variants.question: Which Soviet probe crashed on the Moon while Apollo 11 astronauts were still on the surface?

The Apollo 11 Lunar Module was named ___, and the Command Module was named Columbia.

visualDescription: Two scenes split by the lunar horizon: on one side, an American astronaut plants a flag; on the other, a Soviet probe plummets toward a crater in a flash of debris.
